name: Kaunas Castle
region: Kaunas, confluence of the Nemunas and Neris rivers
architectural_style: Gothic brick river-confluence fortress — a rectangular enclosure with round towers at the corners, walls up to 2.5 metres thick, built at the precise geographic point where the Nemunas and Neris rivers meet; the construction materials (brick manufactured locally from the Kaunas clay deposits) and the structural approach (thick walls designed to resist direct assault and siege machinery rather than artillery) reflect mid-14th-century military engineering; approximately one-third of the original circuit survives, primarily the western tower and sections of the curtain wall; the surviving structure is the first example of brick military construction in the Grand Duchy of Lithuania's documented building history

opening_hours.seasonal_note: Opening hours may vary seasonally; confirm current times at the castle or via the Kaunas city tourism portal before visiting. The GYG day tour from Vilnius covering Kaunas Castle and three Nemunas River valley castles (t1402021, 10 hours, small group max 7, from $227, provider rating) departs from Vilnius and includes a guided stop at the castle — see gyg_featured_tours for details.

visit_duration: 1–1.5 hours (castle and museum); 10 hours (GYG full Nemunas River Castles circuit from Vilnius)
best_season: May to September — the castle's riverside setting is pleasant in warm weather; the museum and castle ruins are accessible year-round but the surrounding historic Old Town of Kaunas (directly adjacent) is at its best in summer and early autumn
accessibility: The surviving castle remains include the western round tower and sections of the curtain wall. The museum space at ground level is accessible. The tower staircase and upper rampart sections involve steps. The Old Town of Kaunas surrounding the castle is broadly accessible at street level.
tagline: The first brick castle the Grand Duchy of Lithuania ever built — raised at the Nemunas-Neris confluence specifically to hold the Teutonic Order at the gates
highlights: First brick castle in the Grand Duchy of Lithuania — the historical significance of Kaunas Castle is architectural as well as military: this was the first time Lithuanian builders employed brick (rather than timber and earthworks) as the primary construction material for a military installation, a technical and organizational achievement that predated Lithuania's full Christianization and its integration into the European brick-building tradition

The strategic position at the Nemunas-Neris confluence — the castle was not placed here by accident; the convergence of the Grand Duchy's two most important rivers created a bottleneck that any army moving from western Prussia into the Lithuanian heartland had to navigate; the castle controlled the crossing point and gave the medieval city of Kaunas its reason to exist in its particular location

The 1362 Teutonic Order siege — the most historically documented event in the castle's early history; Teutonic Knights besieged the new fortress while Lithuanian forces were engaged elsewhere; Prince Vaidotas commanded the defense for a prolonged period before the siege succeeded; the event defined the castle's military purpose and demonstrated the Teutonic Order's strategic reach into Lithuanian territory during the peak of the Baltic Crusades

Post-Grunwald decline into peaceful use — after the Battle of Grunwald in 1410 broke the Teutonic Order's military power definitively, Kaunas Castle's strategic function evaporated; the following centuries were spent as a venue for diplomatic meetings and the residence of the local governor, reflecting the broader shift of medieval Lithuanian military investment from river-junction castles to the consolidated political structures of the Grand Duchy

GYG Kaunas & Nemunas River Castles day tour from Vilnius (t1402021, from $227, max 7 guests, 10 hours) — a small-group guided circuit from Vilnius covering all four Nemunas valley castles: Kaunas Castle, Raudondvaris Manor, Raudonė Castle, and Panemunė Castle in a single day
description:
Kaunas Castle stands at the precise point where the Nemunas and Neris rivers converge — the geographic bottleneck that determined where the city of Kaunas would be built and why a castle there mattered enough to make it the first brick military structure the Grand Duchy of Lithuania ever constructed. The castle's significance is double: strategic, as the fortress controlling the most critical river junction in medieval Lithuania, and architectural, as the first use of fired brick in Lithuanian military building — a technical and organizational achievement that preceded the Grand Duchy's full adoption of European building practices.

The construction of Kaunas Castle is associated with the early 1360s, the period when the Grand Duchy of Lithuania was in sustained conflict with the Teutonic Order — the German military-religious order that had been granted control of Prussia by the Holy Roman Empire and had been steadily pressing eastward into Lithuanian and Latvian territory since the 13th century. The Baltic Crusades, as the series of campaigns against the pagan Lithuanian and Latvian peoples was called, created a persistent military pressure on the Grand Duchy's western frontier for nearly two centuries. Kaunas, as the most accessible deep penetration point via the Nemunas River valley from the Teutonic Order's territory in Prussia, required a permanent defensive installation capable of slowing or stopping an army moving upstream.

The castle that was built to address this requirement was a rectangular brick enclosure with round towers at the corners — a standard European military design of the mid-14th century, adapted to the specific geography of the river junction site. The walls were up to 2.5 metres thick, designed to resist direct assault and the siege machinery of the period. The total enclosed area was approximately 5,200 square metres. The materials were fired brick manufactured locally from the clay deposits around Kaunas — the same material that would later define the architectural character of Kaunas's historic center.

The military value of the new construction was tested almost immediately. In 1362, Teutonic Knights besieged Kaunas Castle while Lithuania's main military forces were engaged elsewhere. The chronicle record of this event names Prince Vaidotas as the commander of the castle's defense — a rare personal attribution in the fragmentary documentation of 14th-century Lithuanian military history. The garrison held for a prolonged period, but the siege ultimately succeeded; the Teutonic Order took the castle after the extended investment. The loss was a significant symbolic blow as well as a tactical one: the first brick castle of the Grand Duchy had fallen within a few years of its construction.

The strategic calculus changed permanently fifty years later. The Battle of Grunwald in 1410 — a combined Polish-Lithuanian victory over the Teutonic Order that remains one of the largest battles of the medieval period — broke the Order's military capacity to mount the kind of large-scale campaigns that had been threatening the Grand Duchy's western frontier since the 13th century. After Grunwald, Kaunas Castle ceased to be a front-line military installation and shifted into the quieter role of an administrative center: a venue for diplomatic meetings, a residence for the local governor, an occasional assembly point for regional administration. The castle's significance became historical rather than strategic.

The physical survival of the castle is partial: approximately one-third of the original circuit remains, predominantly the western round tower and sections of the adjacent curtain wall. The eastern portions were damaged by flooding and gradual deterioration over the centuries of reduced maintenance that followed the castle's obsolescence as a military structure. What survives has been consolidated and is now presented as a museum, with the visible masonry of the 14th-century brick construction accessible to visitors.

Kaunas Castle is the starting point of the Nemunas River Castles circuit — the chain of fortifications and manor houses along the Nemunas and Nevėžis rivers that represents Lithuania's most concentrated assembly of medieval and early modern defensive architecture. [Raudondvaris Manor](/castles/lithuania/raudondvaris-manor), 9 kilometres to the northwest on the Nevėžis River, was the Teutonic Order's Königsburg before Queen Barbara Radziwiłł made it her own. [Raudonė Castle](/castles/lithuania/raudone-castle), further down the Nemunas toward the sea, preserves a 34-metre Renaissance tower climbable as an observation platform. [Panemunė Castle](/castles/lithuania/panemune-castle), the westernmost of the four, was built from 1604 and confiscated by the Tsarist authorities after its owner led a failed 19th-century uprising against Russia.

For Lithuania's other principal fortifications: [Trakai Island Castle](/castles/lithuania/trakai-island-castle), 90 kilometres northeast, is the Grand Duchy's most visually striking medieval castle, built on an island in Lake Galvė. [Gediminas Tower](/castles/lithuania/gediminas-tower) in Vilnius is the upper castle of the Grand Duchy's capital, dating from the early 14th century.
history: c.1360s: Construction of Kaunas Castle begins — the first brick military structure in the Grand Duchy of Lithuania, built at the Nemunas-Neris river confluence to defend against the Teutonic Order. 1362: Teutonic Knights besiege the castle under Prince Vaidotas's defense; the siege succeeds and the Teutonic Order takes the castle. Late 14th century: Castle returns to Lithuanian control. 1410: Battle of Grunwald breaks Teutonic Order military power; Kaunas Castle's front-line strategic function ends. 15th–18th century: Castle used as an administrative center — diplomatic meetings, governors' residence; physical fabric gradually deteriorates. Post-18th century: Partial flooding and structural loss; approximately two-thirds of the original circuit lost. Present day: Surviving western tower and curtain wall sections consolidated and opened as a museum; the first brick castle of Lithuania preserved as a historic monument.
how_to_visit:
**Direct entry (museum, small admission fee):** Walk to the castle from the Kaunas Old Town — the castle is on Pilies Street at the Nemunas-Neris confluence, a 5-minute walk from the Town Hall Square. Check current admission prices and hours at the castle or the Kaunas Municipal Museum website.

**GYG Nemunas River Castles day tour from Vilnius (~$227, t1402021, 10 hours, max 7 guests):** A small-group guided circuit covering all four Nemunas valley castles (Kaunas, Raudondvaris, Raudonė, Panemunė) plus Kaunas city; departs from Vilnius with lunch included. Book well in advance due to small group size.

**Getting there:** Kaunas is 100km from Vilnius (about 90 minutes by train or car). The castle is in the Old Town of Kaunas, 10 minutes on foot from the main train/bus stations.

faqs.question: What makes Kaunas Castle historically significant beyond Lithuania?
faqs.answer: Kaunas Castle represents two firsts: it was the first structure in the Grand Duchy of Lithuania to use fired brick as the primary building material for a military fortification, and it was the front-line bulwark against the Teutonic Order at the most strategically critical river junction in medieval Lithuania. The Teutonic Order's siege of 1362 is one of the best-documented events in 14th-century Baltic military history, making Kaunas Castle an important reference point in the broader history of the Baltic Crusades — the century-long conflict between the German military-religious orders and the last pagan state in medieval Europe.

faqs.question: Why does the castle survive only partially?
faqs.answer: After the Battle of Grunwald in 1410 ended the Teutonic Order's military threat, Kaunas Castle ceased to be a front-line installation and received progressively less maintenance. Flooding from the Nemunas and Neris rivers, combined with centuries of reduced upkeep, destroyed approximately two-thirds of the original circuit. The surviving western tower and curtain wall sections were eventually consolidated and protected; the eastern portions were lost to water damage and structural collapse over several centuries.
